metadata.description: Comprehensive generation technology database aligned with the Open Energy Ontology (OEO). Designed for use with PyPSA, Calliope, OSeMOSYS, and ADOPTNet0 modeling frameworks. Covers small-scale PV (300 Wp balcony systems) through utility-scale solar, onshore wind (community single-turbine to 600 MW US Plains), fixed-bottom offshore wind (Baltic nearshore to 1.5 GW gigafarm), and floating offshore wind (Hywind Norway spar to 1 GW next-generation projections). Includes Germany-specific EEG, Bürgerwindpark, and North Sea EEZ contexts.
primary_sources: US EIA Capital Cost and Performance Characteristic Estimates 2020
HTW Berlin Yield Study – Steckersolare Systeme 2022

technologies.description: Large-scale ground-mounted photovoltaic power plants connected directly to transmission networks, typically >1 MW. Includes single-axis tracking systems.

technologies.description: Small to medium-scale rooftop or ground-mounted PV systems connected to distribution networks. Covers nano-residential (1–2 kW), residential (3–20 kW), small commercial (20–200 kW), commercial (200–500 kW), and industrial (0.5–5 MW) segments. Module technologies include PERC, TOPCon, and HJT.

technologies.description: Plug-in photovoltaic micro-generation units mounted on balconies, facades, terraces, or flat-roof parapets. Connected directly to the household circuit via a Schuko socket (EU) or equivalent. Typically 150–2000 W peak. Legally regulated in Germany under DIN VDE 0100-551-1 with a maximum feed-in of 800 W (2024 reform from 600 W). No grid operator notification required for systems ≤ 2 kW peak / ≤ 800 W inverter output in Germany.

technologies.description: Land-based wind turbines from single-turbine community installations (2–3 MW) to large utility farms (600+ MW). Covers six turbine classes: micro/community (2–3 MW, hub height 80–100 m), small commercial (3–5 MW), mid-size (5–7 MW, modern German standard), large modern (7–10 MW), next-generation (>10 MW). Capacity factors range from ~25% in low-wind interior sites to ~55% on US Great Plains. Includes German Bürgerwindpark, EEG auction, and repowering contexts.

generation_profile.notes: Example hourly capacity-factor profile for an onshore wind fleet with moderate winter seasonality. Not site-specific.

technologies.description: Offshore wind turbines on fixed-bottom foundations (monopile, jacket, tripod, gravity-based) in water depths up to ~60 m. Turbine sizes range from 8 MW (2020s legacy) to 20+ MW (next-generation). Covers nearshore Baltic-type projects (<30 km, <30 m depth, jacket/monopile), standard North Sea farms (30–60 m, monopile), and gigafarm configurations. Specific turbine references include Siemens Gamesa SG 14-222 DD, Vestas V236-15, and GE Haliade-X 14 MW. Includes German EEZ, repowering, and multi-year cost projections.

technologies.description: Offshore wind turbines on floating sub-structures for water depths >60 m. Three platform types: spar-buoy (ballast stabilised, suitable for >120 m, e.g. Hywind Norway), semi-submersible (column-stabilised, deployable from 60 m, most commercial pipeline), and tension-leg platform (TLP, mooring-stabilised, lower motion, 60–200 m). Enables access to deep-water, high-wind resources unreachable by fixed-bottom foundations. CAPEX declines sharply post-2028 as supply chains, installation vessels, and port infrastructure mature. Capacity factors are typically higher than fixed-bottom due to superior wind resources at deep-water sites.
